title: Pool lifecycle actions appear callable with attacker-forged parameters
reason: Unsupported by the available source: `FlawVerifier` only issues unchecked low-level calls to an external address and ignores the return values, so the code does not show that `startPool`/`endPool` actually succeed for attacker-chosen parameters.

title: Pools can apparently be started and ended in the same transaction
reason: Same issue as above: the PoC attempts back-to-back calls, but without the target contract or execution evidence there is no proof that both calls can succeed in one transaction.

title: Predictable pool parameter space enables protocol-wide brute-force sweeping
reason: The hardcoded loops only show one guessed search strategy inside the PoC; they do not establish that the underlying protocol keys pools solely by these parameters or that enumeration is actually sufficient to sweep real pools.

title: The exploit entrypoint is permissionless and trivially front-runnable
reason: Anyone can call `executeOnOpportunity()`, but the caller does not receive the proceeds; front-running only triggers the same contract path and is largely subsumed by the existing locked-funds issue rather than creating a distinct theft vector.

title: The exhaustive 1,800-call sweep can become unexecutable from gas exhaustion
reason: Too speculative and mainly affects this PoC's own execution path. The available code does not show the callee gas cost, and any failure here does not by itself demonstrate protocol-level fund loss or permissionless DoS.

title: Counter state is completely attacker-controlled
reason: `Counter.sol` is a standalone toy/template contract with no integration shown anywhere else in the codebase; unrestricted setters here are expected and not a reportable protocol issue on the available evidence.
